h1Title: Acute Bronchiolitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute bronchiolitis is a viral infection of the small bronchiolar airways causing mucosal inflammation, edema, and luminal obstruction that produces wheeze, crackles, and respiratory distress predominantly in infants and young children under 2 years. Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) causes 50-80% of all cases; human metapneumovirus, rhinovirus, parainfluenza, and adenovirus account for most of the rest. Bronchiolitis is the leading cause of infant hospitalization in the United States and UK: approximately 100,000-150,000 admissions per year in the US alone. The disease is self-limiting; 97% of cases resolve without specific antiviral therapy. Treatment is supportive — supplemental oxygen and nasogastric or intravenous fluids — and neither bronchodilators, corticosteroids, nor antibiotics improve outcomes in randomized trials.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 2-3% of all infants in their first year of life annually; 100,000-150,000 US hospitalizations per year (CDC data); peak in winter RSV season
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects children under 2; peak incidence at age 2-6 months when maternal antibody protection is waning; boys slightly more affected than girls (ratio ~1.25:1)
keyStats.avgAge: Peak severity and hospitalization at age 2-6 months; uncommon above age 2 years in immunocompetent children; rare in adults except in immunosuppressed patients
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 33 million episodes of RSV lower respiratory tract disease annually in children under 5, causing approximately 120,000 deaths per year, predominantly in low- and middle-income countries (Shi et al., Lancet 2017)

definition: Acute bronchiolitis (ICD-10: J21.9 for unspecified etiology) is an acute viral lower respiratory tract illness affecting the bronchioles — the smallest airways above the alveoli — predominantly in infants and children under 24 months. Viral infection of the bronchiolar epithelium causes inflammatory cell infiltration, mucus hypersecretion, epithelial necrosis, and edema, resulting in partial or complete luminal obstruction. The resulting air-trapping and ventilation-perfusion mismatch produce the characteristic wheeze, prolonged expiratory phase, hyperinflated chest, and hypoxemia. Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is responsible for 50-80% of cases, making bronchiolitis and RSV lower respiratory tract disease essentially synonymous in clinical practice. Secondary bacterial infection is rare in bronchiolitis — fewer than 2% of hospitalized infants have a concurrent serious bacterial infection — making antibiotic therapy inappropriate in the vast majority. The disease is managed by pediatrics, general practice, and emergency medicine. The primary clinical challenge is identifying the minority of infants who require hospitalization for respiratory support, while managing the majority at home with safety-net advice.

typesClassification.description: The dominant subtype, accounting for 50-80% of cases. Peak incidence November to March in Northern Hemisphere. RSV bronchiolitis can be severe in premature infants, those under 3 months, and those with congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease of prematurity, or immunodeficiency. RSV-specific prevention (nirsevimab monoclonal antibody) is now available.

Human metapneumovirus (hMPV) causes 10-20% of bronchiolitis and is clinically indistinguishable from RSV disease. Rhinovirus, adenovirus, parainfluenza, influenza, and bocavirus account for the remainder. Adenovirus bronchiolitis can be severe and cause obliterative bronchiolitis (chronic sequela) in a small subset.

Acute viral bronchiolitis in adults is rare in immunocompetent individuals but occurs in the elderly and immunosuppressed. Non-infectious causes include toxic fume inhalation and connective tissue disease. Adult obliterative bronchiolitis is a distinct chronic entity from post-lung-transplant rejection or inhalation injury.

primarySymptoms: Wheeze — a high-pitched or low-pitched expiratory noise caused by air forced through partially obstructed small bronchioles. In bronchiolitis, wheeze is diffuse throughout both lung fields, distinguishing it from the focal monophonic wheeze of a foreign body.
Subcostal and intercostal recessions — inward drawing of the spaces between ribs and below the ribcage during inspiration, indicating significant increased work of breathing and airway obstruction.

Tachypnea — respiratory rate above 50 breaths per minute in infants under 2 months, above 45 in 2-12 months, and above 40 in older infants. Elevated respiratory rate is the single most sensitive sign of significant lower respiratory illness in infants.
Crackles (fine crepitations) on chest auscultation — caused by sudden re-opening of collapsed peripheral airways during inspiration; often bilateral and best heard at the lung bases.
Nasal secretions and congestion — rhinorrhea begins 1-3 days before lower respiratory symptoms in nearly all cases, marking the upper-to-lower respiratory virus progression.
Fever — low-grade (37.5-38.5°C) in most cases; high fever above 39°C is uncommon in typical viral bronchiolitis and raises consideration of bacterial co-infection or influenza.

Poor feeding — infants with bronchiolitis tire from the increased work of breathing during sucking and reduce feed volumes by 25-50%. Poor feeding is a key trigger for hospitalization when dehydration threatens.

Apnea — brief pauses in breathing most common in very young infants (under 6 weeks), premature infants, and those with RSV bronchiolitis. Apnea may be the first sign of RSV illness in neonates before respiratory symptoms develop.
Hyperinflated, barrel-shaped chest — caused by air trapping distal to bronchiolar obstructions; visible clinically and on chest X-ray as hyperexpanded lung fields with flattened diaphragm.
earlyWarningSigns: Progression from runny nose to noisy, fast breathing within 2-4 days in an infant aged 2-12 months during RSV season — this temporal sequence is almost pathognomonic for bronchiolitis.

Increasing difficulty feeding — a baby taking less than 50% of normal feeds or showing significant fatigue at the breast or bottle is approaching the threshold for intravenous or nasogastric fluid support.
Respiratory rate of 50-60 breaths per minute at rest with mild subcostal recession — indicates evolving airway obstruction requiring early clinical review.
Intermittent brief apneic pauses in a young infant with upper respiratory symptoms — RSV apnea can precede wheeze and crackles by hours in neonates under 6 weeks.
emergencySigns: Oxygen saturation below 92% on room air — requires immediate supplemental oxygen and emergency department assessment; below 90% in an infant with bronchiolitis indicates impending respiratory failure.

Severe respiratory distress with subcostal, intercostal, and sternal recession plus head-bobbing or nasal flaring — signs of a high respiratory load exhausting the infant's reserve; intensive care assessment needed.
Apnea lasting more than 20 seconds or apnea associated with colour change (cyanosis, pallor) — requires immediate airway management and emergency call; high-flow oxygen or CPAP may be needed.
Cyanosis of lips or tongue — indicates severe hypoxemia and demands immediate oxygen supplementation and emergency care.

Toxic appearance with temperature above 39.5°C and respiratory distress in an infant under 3 months — raises concern for bacterial co-infection or sepsis requiring urgent blood cultures and empirical antibiotics.

causes.description: RSV infects and destroys bronchiolar ciliated epithelial cells, causing edema, mucus plugging, and necrotic debris in the small airways. This produces the characteristic air trapping and wheeze. RSV spreads via large droplets and direct contact with contaminated surfaces; it survives for 4-7 hours on hard surfaces. All children are infected at least once by age 2, but primary infection in the first year carries the highest risk of severe bronchiolitis.

Closely related to RSV in structure and clinical presentation. Accounts for 10-20% of bronchiolitis episodes and may co-infect with RSV in particularly severe cases. Peak incidence late winter to spring. Unlike RSV, no approved vaccine or monoclonal antibody prophylaxis exists for hMPV.

Rhinovirus is the most common cause of common colds in all ages and accounts for 10-15% of bronchiolitis episodes. Rhinovirus bronchiolitis tends to be less severe than RSV disease in infants without underlying risk factors; however, it is a major trigger of subsequent wheezing episodes and may unmask underlying atopic airway disease in susceptible infants.

Parainfluenza type 3 is most likely to produce bronchiolitis in infants; types 1 and 2 more commonly cause croup in older children. Parainfluenza bronchiolitis peaks in autumn. Severe disease is most common in infants under 6 months and those with chronic lung disease of prematurity.

Adenovirus causes a minority of bronchiolitis cases but is associated with the most severe outcomes — including necrotizing bronchiolitis that can progress to obliterative bronchiolitis with permanent lung damage. Children under 2 years with adenoviral bronchiolitis have higher rates of respiratory failure and mechanical ventilation than those with RSV disease.

riskFactors.description: Very young infants have the smallest bronchiolar diameter — even modest edema causes disproportionate luminal narrowing. In Poiseuille's law terms, resistance increases as the inverse of the fourth power of the radius; a 50% reduction in diameter increases resistance 16-fold. Infants under 3 months also have immature respiratory control, increasing apnea risk.

Premature infants have smaller airways, less mature immunological defences, and often have chronic lung disease of prematurity (bronchopulmonary dysplasia). They have 3-4 times higher hospitalization rates from RSV bronchiolitis than term infants. Nirsevimab prophylaxis is recommended for all premature infants in their first RSV season.

Infants with haemodynamically significant CHD — particularly those with left-to-right shunts and pulmonary hypertension — tolerate RSV bronchiolitis poorly. They have 3-4 times higher risk of mechanical ventilation and mortality compared with healthy infants. Nirsevimab prophylaxis is recommended for the first two RSV seasons.

Household tobacco smoking (active or passive) increases bronchiolitis severity and hospitalization risk by approximately 50-70%. Maternal smoking during pregnancy increases risk via impaired fetal lung development. Complete smoke-free households significantly reduce severity.

Breastfeeding provides passive transfer of maternal RSV-specific IgA antibodies and other immune factors. Exclusively breastfed infants have 40-50% lower risk of severe RSV bronchiolitis compared with formula-fed infants in multiple cohort studies.

riskFactors.factor: Crowded living conditions or daycare attendance
riskFactors.description: RSV spreads efficiently via direct contact; crowding and daycare dramatically increase RSV exposure risk. Sibling attendance at school or daycare is an important transmission pathway for RSV into the household to reach the youngest infant.

Children with primary immunodeficiency or receiving chemotherapy are at risk of prolonged, severe, and potentially fatal bronchiolitis. RSV can persist for weeks in immunocompromised children and adults, causing progressive respiratory failure.

affectedDemographics: Infants aged 2-6 months at peak risk for severe hospitalized bronchiolitis; near-universal primary RSV infection by age 24 months.
Premature infants — defined as born before 36 weeks gestation — have 3-4 times the hospitalization rate and higher ICU admission rate than term infants.
Male infants are modestly more affected than female infants with a sex ratio of approximately 1.25:1 across multiple epidemiological studies.
Low-income families and those in crowded housing or using communal daycare have higher exposure and hospitalization rates.
diagnosisOverview: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic history and examination findings. No laboratory test or imaging is required for uncomplicated cases in otherwise healthy infants. The diagnosis is established by: age under 24 months, first episode of wheeze with or without crackles, preceding upper respiratory symptoms (runny nose, mild fever) for 1-3 days, seasonal timing during autumn-winter RSV season, and no features suggesting alternative diagnoses. The 2021 AAP (American Academy of Pediatrics) guideline on bronchiolitis emphasizes a positive diagnosis of bronchiolitis rather than exclusion of other diagnoses through testing. Chest X-ray is not recommended routinely: it shows hyperinflation and peribronchial thickening in bronchiolitis but frequently misinterprets atelectasis as consolidation, leading to unnecessary antibiotic prescribing. Chest X-ray should be reserved for infants with severe respiratory distress, failure to improve as expected, or clinical uncertainty about an alternative diagnosis. Viral PCR from a nasopharyngeal swab (RSV antigen or multiplex respiratory panel) is not required for clinical management of typical bronchiolitis but is useful for cohorting purposes in hospital wards to prevent nosocomial spread. Oxygen saturation measurement is the single most important objective measure — persistent SpO2 below 92-94% identifies infants needing admission. Urine dipstick and full blood count are not recommended unless specific indications for bacterial infection are present.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination and vital signs assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic tool. Establishes respiratory rate, heart rate, temperature, oxygen saturation, degree of recession, presence of wheeze and crackles, and feeding assessment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A physical examination by a nurse or doctor, including observation of breathing pattern, use of accessory muscles, skin colour, and chest auscultation. Takes 5-10 minutes. SpO2 is measured by a painless probe clipped to a finger or toe.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The key objective measure guiding admission decision. SpO2 persistently below 92-94% on room air in a comfortably breathing infant is the primary threshold for supplemental oxygen and hospital admission
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small plastic probe with a red light is clipped to the baby's toe or finger for 30-60 seconds. Painless. Reading displayed immediately. Critical for safe triage decisions.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasopharyngeal RSV antigen test or multiplex viral PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms RSV etiology for infection control cohorting on hospital wards; not required to diagnose or manage bronchiolitis in outpatients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin swab is gently inserted through one nostril to the nasopharynx — briefly uncomfortable, lasting 5-10 seconds. RSV rapid antigen result in 15 minutes; multiplex PCR in 2-6 hours. Does not change clinical management.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical cases, severe respiratory distress, or clinical suspicion of alternative diagnoses — not a routine investigation in bronchiolitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 2-minute X-ray with the infant held still by a parent or radiographer. Mild distress from being held for positioning. Typical bronchiolitis shows hyperinflated lungs and peribronchial thickening, not consolidation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Infants with poor feeding and prolonged illness are at risk of hypoglycemia; a heel-prick glucose check is indicated in infants admitted with significant feeding difficulty
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief heel-prick capillary blood glucose test, giving a result in 30 seconds. Performed by a nurse using a lancet device. Identifies the rare infant needing dextrose supplementation.

treatmentOverview: Acute bronchiolitis treatment is supportive — there are no approved antiviral or anti-inflammatory therapies that alter its natural course in the general infant population. The 2021 American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) clinical practice guideline on bronchiolitis and the 2015 NICE guideline (NG9) both recommend against the routine use of bronchodilators (salbutamol, epinephrine), inhaled or systemic corticosteroids, antibiotics, chest physiotherapy, or nebulized hypertonic saline in previously healthy infants. Despite this evidence, these treatments continue to be widely used outside guideline-adherent centers. Supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula is administered when SpO2 falls persistently below 90-94%; the threshold varies by guideline but most centers maintain SpO2 at or above 92-94%. High-flow nasal cannula oxygen therapy (HFNC) at flows of 1-2 L/kg/min provides humidified heated oxygen, reduces respiratory distress scores, and reduces escalation to intensive care in moderate-severe bronchiolitis — HFNC has become standard in pediatric wards at many centers, though the PARIS RCT (2019) showed it reduced ICU transfers without improving time to clinical stability. Nasogastric tube feeding is initiated when oral feed intake falls below 50-60% of normal and the infant is at risk of dehydration; intravenous fluids are reserved for the most severely affected infants who cannot tolerate nasogastric feeds. Primary prevention of RSV bronchiolitis was revolutionized by the approval of nirsevimab (Beyfortus) in 2022-2023 — a single intramuscular dose passive antibody providing protection for a full RSV season, now recommended for all infants in their first RSV season in the US, UK, and Europe.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula (0.5-2 L/min)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard supportive care for SpO2 persistently below 90-94% on room air. Delivered by low-flow nasal prongs in alert infants who are feeding adequately. Titrated to maintain SpO2 above 92-94% without causing hyperoxia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects hypoxemia within minutes; indirect reduction in apnea risk; does not shorten illness duration but prevents hypoxic end-organ injury

medicalTreatments.name: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen (HFNC) at 1-2 L/kg/min
medicalTreatments.description: Warmed humidified oxygen at high flow rates providing positive end-expiratory pressure effect, reducing work of breathing and improving alveolar recruitment. Standard treatment in moderate-severe bronchiolitis requiring hospitalization in most pediatric centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces ICU transfers compared with low-flow O2 in moderate bronchiolitis (PARIS RCT 2019); reduces respiratory rate and distress scores within 1-2 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Nasogastric tube feeding
medicalTreatments.description: When infants are feeding less than 50-60% of usual volumes due to respiratory distress or fatigue, nasogastric tube placement allows enteral fluid and nutrition delivery without the energy expenditure of sucking. Avoids the need for IV access in most infants who require fluid support.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents dehydration; allows safe nutrition delivery; reduces length of hospital stay compared with IV fluids in studies where nasogastric feeding is used early

medicalTreatments.name: Saline nasal drops and suctioning
medicalTreatments.description: 0.9% saline nasal drops followed by gentle bulb or low-vacuum nasal suctioning clears anterior nasal secretions and can improve comfort and feeding immediately before each feed in congested infants. Deep suctioning is avoided as it can worsen mucosal trauma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves upper airway patency transiently; reduces nasopharyngeal obstruction at feeding time; does not alter the lower respiratory component of bronchiolitis or shorten illness

alternativeTreatments.name: Nebulized hypertonic saline (3%) in selected inpatients
alternativeTreatments.description: Some pediatric guidelines allow a trial of 3% hypertonic saline nebulization in hospitalized infants as it may increase mucociliary clearance. The 2020 Cochrane meta-analysis showed modest reduction in length of hospital stay (approximately 10 hours). Not recommended for outpatient use and not universally adopted.

whySeeSpecialist: Most bronchiolitis is managed by general practitioners and emergency physicians without subspecialty involvement. Pediatric pulmonology or respiratory medicine input is appropriate for infants with recurrent bronchiolitis episodes (more than three), for those who fail to recover as expected, or for atypical presentations that raise concern for underlying conditions such as congenital heart disease, tracheomalacia, or immune deficiency.
doctorSelectionGuide: For a typical first bronchiolitis episode in a previously healthy term infant over 6 weeks, assessment by a GP or pediatric emergency nurse practitioner is appropriate. Seek pediatric emergency department assessment when the infant has SpO2 below 95%, respiratory rate above 60, significant subcostal recession, feeding below 50% of normal, or age under 6 weeks. Infants with high-risk conditions (prematurity, CHD) should be seen promptly at the first sign of lower respiratory symptoms.
hospitalCriteria: SpO2 persistently below 92-94% on room air requiring supplemental oxygen
Respiratory rate above 70 per minute or severe recession indicating high work of breathing
Feeding under 50% of usual volumes with clinical signs of dehydration
Age under 6 weeks or premature birth below 32 weeks gestation with any respiratory symptoms
keyFacilities: Pediatric emergency departments with HFNC capability
Pediatric wards with nasogastric feeding and continuous SpO2 monitoring
Pediatric intensive care units for infants requiring CPAP or mechanical ventilation
Neonatal units for bronchiolitis in infants under 4 weeks
preventionStrategies: Administer nirsevimab (Beyfortus) — a single intramuscular dose RSV monoclonal antibody recommended for all infants entering their first RSV season, with priority for premature infants and those with congenital heart disease or chronic lung disease.

Breastfeed exclusively for at least the first 4-6 months of life — breastfeeding reduces the risk of severe RSV bronchiolitis by 40-50% through passive transfer of maternal antibodies and anti-inflammatory immune factors.
Avoid tobacco smoke exposure — implement a completely smoke-free home and car environment; maternal cessation during pregnancy improves fetal lung development.
Practice regular handwashing with soap and water, especially after contact with anyone with a respiratory illness, and before handling young infants.
Reduce daycare exposure during RSV season for high-risk infants under 3 months when feasible — delay start of group childcare until after the first RSV season for premature infants.
lifestyleModifications: Elevate the head of the infant's cot by 15-30 degrees to reduce nasal secretion drainage into the nasopharynx and improve comfort during respiratory distress.
Use saline nasal drops (2-3 drops per nostril) before each feed to clear congestion and reduce the work of breathing during feeding.
Offer frequent small feeds (every 1-2 hours rather than scheduled larger feeds) to prevent dehydration while reducing the duration of each sucking effort.
Keep the infant's environment smoke-free and avoid other respiratory irritants such as aerosols, incense, and strong chemical smells.
Monitor respiratory rate and feeding volume daily; seek medical review if respiratory rate rises above 60 per minute at rest or feeds drop below 50% of usual volume.

recommended: Breast milk as first-choice nutrition — optimal for immune support and reduced bronchiolitis severity
Frequent small feeds to maintain hydration when breathing effort makes prolonged feeding difficult
Clear fluids or oral rehydration solution as temporary bridge when milk feeds are poorly tolerated during peak respiratory distress
Continuing age-appropriate solid foods in infants already weaned, adapting texture if swallowing is impaired during illness
avoid: Large volume infrequent feeds that fatigue an already breathing-stressed infant
Honey and herbal preparations in infants under 12 months — unsafe due to botulism risk in honey and unstudied safety of herbal remedies in neonates
Over-the-counter cold and cough medications, antihistamines, and decongestants — not safe or effective in infants under 2 years
Cow's milk formula substituted for breast milk without medical guidance during acute illness

exerciseGuidelines: Not applicable for infants during the acute phase. Rest and minimal handling reduce metabolic oxygen demand. Once recovered — typically within 2-4 weeks — normal infant activity resumes. Caregivers should be advised that mild exertional wheeze may persist for 1-2 weeks after discharge as bronchial hyper-reactivity resolves.
dailyManagement: Count respiratory breaths for one full minute at rest twice daily; alert the medical team if rate exceeds 60 per minute in hospital or at home.
Measure SpO2 with the home monitor (if provided on discharge) and report readings below 92% immediately.
Use nasal saline drops and gentle suctioning before each feed to clear congestion and improve feeding tolerance.
Weigh wet nappies — at least 4 wet nappies in 24 hours confirms adequate hydration; fewer than 4 requires urgent medical review.
Keep the infant upright or semi-reclined during and for 30 minutes after feeds to reduce reflux that can worsen cough and respiratory distress.
prognosis: Prognosis for acute bronchiolitis in previously healthy term infants is excellent. The illness is self-limiting: symptoms peak on days 2-5, and most infants return to normal feeding and breathing within 1-2 weeks. Hospital admission is required in approximately 2-3% of all cases; of admitted infants, roughly 5-10% need high-dependency or intensive care. Death from bronchiolitis in healthy term infants in high-income countries is rare — estimated mortality is below 1 per 1,000 admitted infants. Premature infants and those with congenital heart disease or chronic lung disease of prematurity have substantially higher rates of ICU admission and a mortality risk of 1-3% when hospitalized. A clinically important long-term sequela is the association between RSV bronchiolitis in infancy and subsequent recurrent wheeze: approximately 30-50% of infants hospitalized with bronchiolitis will have at least one episode of wheeze in the following 3 years, and 10-15% will develop persistent asthma by school age. Whether RSV itself causes this or whether susceptibility to both bronchiolitis and wheeze reflects a shared genetic predisposition (the 'early wheeze phenotype') remains an active research question.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms peak at days 2-5 of illness. SpO2 typically normalizes by day 5-7 in admitted infants. Feeding volume returns to normal by day 5-10 in most cases. Wheeze and crackles on examination resolve by 2 weeks in 90% of infants. Mild nasal congestion and occasional cough may persist for 2-4 weeks. Full return to baseline respiratory status is expected by 4 weeks in healthy term infants.
complications: Apnea — the most dangerous acute complication in infants under 6 weeks or those born prematurely; RSV is a leading cause of apparent life-threatening events in neonates; requires hospital monitoring during the acute phase.
Respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation — occurs in approximately 3-5% of admitted infants; most common in premature infants, those with CHD, and those with adenoviral bronchiolitis.
Otitis media — acute middle ear infection complicates 30-50% of bronchiolitis episodes; bacterial secondary infection is the most common cause of fever persisting beyond day 5.
Recurrent wheeze post-bronchiolitis — 30-50% of infants hospitalized with bronchiolitis develop at least one subsequent wheeze episode; 10-15% develop asthma by school age.
Obliterative bronchiolitis — rare but severe chronic sequela of adenoviral bronchiolitis causing permanent small-airway obliteration and a fixed obstructive pattern on spirometry in older childhood.
Nosocomial RSV transmission in hospital wards — uncohorting of RSV and non-RSV infants can cause iatrogenic spread; cohort RSV-positive infants and use contact precautions.

supportResources.name: NHS — Bronchiolitis in Babies and Children
supportResources.description: NHS patient information on recognizing bronchiolitis, when to seek help, and home management advice for parents.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Pediatrics — Bronchiolitis Clinical Practice Guideline
supportResources.description: The 2014 (reaffirmed 2021) AAP guideline on diagnosis and management of bronchiolitis — freely accessible evidence summary.

supportResources.name: CDC — RSV in Infants and Young Children
supportResources.description: US CDC information on RSV prevention, nirsevimab prophylaxis, and when to seek emergency care for infants.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma is a chronic recurrent condition requiring daily controller therapy, whereas bronchiolitis is typically a single self-limited episode in infants under 2 years. A first wheeze episode in infancy is most likely bronchiolitis — asthma is not reliably diagnosed before age 5. Recurrent wheeze episodes beyond the second year, a family atopy history, or personal eczema suggest early-onset asthma rather than repeated bronchiolitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Bronchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute bronchitis affects older children and adults, involves the large and medium bronchi rather than bronchioles, and presents with cough without the degree of wheeze and airway obstruction seen in bronchiolitis. Bronchiolitis is defined by small-airway involvement in infants under 2 years. In older children and adults, viral lower respiratory infections are classified as acute bronchitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia involves alveolar infection causing lobar or segmental consolidation on chest X-ray, focal crackles, and higher fever. Bronchiolitis causes diffuse bilateral hyperinflation and peribronchial thickening without consolidation. Atelectasis in bronchiolitis may mimic consolidation on X-ray and is a common source of antibiotic over-prescribing; repeat films after physiotherapy typically show resolution of the apparent opacity.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe RSV bronchiolitis in infancy is strongly associated with subsequent recurrent wheeze and asthma development by school age; the shared airway hyper-reactive phenotype is an active research focus.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Bronchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The clinical analogue of bronchiolitis in older children and adults; shares viral etiology and supportive treatment principles but affects larger airways.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: The main clinical differential; RSV can cause both bronchiolitis and pneumonia, with the two conditions overlapping in premature infants and immunocompromised patients.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe adenoviral or RSV bronchiolitis can progress to ARDS in premature and immunocompromised infants, requiring mechanical ventilation and intensive care management.

faqs.question: What is acute bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: Acute bronchiolitis is a viral infection of the small airways (bronchioles) causing inflammation, wheeze, and breathing difficulty, almost exclusively in infants under 2 years. RSV (respiratory syncytial virus) causes the majority of cases. It is the leading cause of infant hospitalization in the US and UK during winter months.

faqs.question: What causes bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: RSV causes 50-80% of bronchiolitis cases. Human metapneumovirus, rhinovirus, parainfluenza, adenovirus, and influenza account for most of the rest. Viruses spread via respiratory droplets and direct contact with contaminated surfaces. All children are infected with RSV at least once by age 2 years.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of bronchiolitis in babies?
faqs.answer: Symptoms begin with a runny nose and mild fever, followed within 2-3 days by wheeze, fast breathing, subcostal recess, and crackles in the chest. Poor feeding, irritability, and in young infants, brief pauses in breathing (apnea) may occur. Symptoms typically peak at days 2-5 and resolve by 2 weeks.

faqs.question: How long does bronchiolitis last?
faqs.answer: Most infants recover within 1-2 weeks. Upper respiratory symptoms appear first and last 5-7 days. Wheeze and fast breathing peak at days 3-5 and resolve by day 10-14 in most cases. Mild cough and nasal congestion can persist for up to 4 weeks. A small number still have wheeze at 3 weeks.

faqs.question: Does bronchiolitis need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Bronchiolitis is caused by viruses and does not respond to antibiotics. Major guidelines from the AAP, NICE, and ERS recommend against routine antibiotic use. Antibiotics are only given for confirmed bacterial co-infection — which occurs in fewer than 2% of bronchiolitis cases.

faqs.question: When should I take my baby to hospital for bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: Take your baby to hospital immediately if oxygen saturation is below 92%, breathing rate is above 60-70 per minute with marked chest recession, the baby is barely feeding (under 50% of normal), lips or tongue look blue, or the baby has pauses in breathing. Babies under 6 weeks with any lower respiratory symptoms should be assessed the same day.

faqs.question: How is bronchiolitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is supportive. Supplemental oxygen is given when oxygen levels fall below 92-94%. Nasogastric tube feeding maintains hydration when babies cannot feed adequately. High-flow nasal cannula oxygen helps in moderate-severe cases. Bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics are not recommended by clinical guidelines.

faqs.question: Is bronchiolitis the same as RSV?
faqs.answer: Not exactly. RSV (respiratory syncytial virus) is the virus that causes most bronchiolitis; bronchiolitis is the clinical illness RSV produces in the lower airways. RSV can also cause milder upper respiratory illness without bronchiolitis. The terms are often used interchangeably, but bronchiolitis can also be caused by other viruses.

faqs.question: Can bronchiolitis lead to asthma?
faqs.answer: Early severe bronchiolitis is associated with a higher risk of recurrent wheeze and asthma by school age. About 30-50% of hospitalized bronchiolitis infants will wheeze again within 3 years. It is unclear whether RSV itself causes airway changes or whether children prone to asthma are also prone to severe bronchiolitis.

faqs.question: What is nirsevimab and does my baby need it?
faqs.answer: Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) is a long-acting RSV monoclonal antibody given as a single injection before a baby's first RSV season. It provides protection for approximately 5 months. It is now recommended for all infants entering their first RSV season in the US, UK, and many European countries. High-risk babies (premature, CHD) are prioritized.

faqs.question: Can adults get bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: Acute viral bronchiolitis of the type seen in infants is rare in immunocompetent adults. Adults can get RSV lower respiratory tract infections, which may produce a bronchiolitis-like illness. Immunocompromised adults — post-transplant, on chemotherapy — can develop severe bronchiolitis from RSV, CMV, or other viruses. A separate condition called obliterative bronchiolitis affects adults after lung transplant.

faqs.question: Is bronchiolitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes, through the virus that causes it. RSV spreads easily via respiratory droplets and by touching contaminated surfaces then the eyes, nose, or mouth. It can survive on hard surfaces for 4-7 hours. Handwashing with soap and water is the most effective way to reduce spread within the family.

faqs.question: Do bronchodilators (blue inhalers) help with bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: Randomized trials consistently show that salbutamol and other bronchodilators do not improve oxygen levels, feeding, or length of hospital stay in typical bronchiolitis. The AAP and NICE guidelines both recommend against routine bronchodilator use. This makes bronchiolitis distinctly different from asthma, where bronchodilators are highly effective.

faqs.question: What are the risk factors for severe bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: Risk factors for severe disease include age under 3 months, premature birth, congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease of prematurity, immunodeficiency, and household tobacco smoke exposure. These infants should be seen promptly at the first sign of lower respiratory symptoms and closely monitored.

faqs.question: How can I prevent my baby getting bronchiolitis?
faqs.answer: Breastfeeding reduces risk by 40-50%. Nirsevimab RSV antibody injection gives strong protection for the first RSV season. Avoiding tobacco smoke, frequent handwashing, and reducing crowded childcare exposure in the first few months of life also reduce risk. There is no RSV vaccine for infants yet, though several are in late-stage trials.

metaTitle: Acute Bronchiolitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Acute bronchiolitis: viral RSV infection of infant airways causing wheeze and breathing difficulty. Symptoms, hospitalization criteria, and treatment reviewed.
